coredata.dc:title: Promotion effects of microwave and carbon dots interactions on hydrolysis for highly efficient hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Chunlin
Liu, Shuai
Wang, Yuxin
Hu, Jianli
Lester, Edward
Wu, Tao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dot-doped Co3O4 was synthesized and used in NaBH4 hydrolysis under both microwave and conventional heating. Microwave energy significantly increased the hydrogen generation rate with a CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst, ranging from 34.1 % at 35 °C to 70.7 % at 55 °C compared with conventional heating. This improvement is due to the combined contribution of microwave thermal and non-thermal effects. These phenomena greatly enhances the effective collision frequency of the reaction species at the interface of the catalyst. Furthermore, the kinetics of NaBH4 hydrolysis with the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst follows a zero-order kinetic model. Under microwave irradiation, the HGR of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst was 4.5 times that of pure Co3O4 at 40 °C. Incorporating carbon dots (CDs) into the Co3O4 matrix simultaneously enhances microwave absorption, increases hydrophilicity, and reduces the activation energy needed for breaking down O–H bonds in H2O. These improvements are expected to boost the overall catalytic performance.

head.ce:title: Promotion effects of microwave and carbon dots interactions on hydrolysis for highly efficient hydrogen production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dot-doped Co3O4 was synthesized and used in NaBH4 hydrolysis under both microwave and conventional heating. Microwave energy significantly increased the hydrogen generation rate with a CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst, ranging from 34.1 % at 35 °C to 70.7 % at 55 °C compared with conventional heating. This improvement is due to the combined contribution of microwave thermal and non-thermal effects. These phenomena greatly enhances the effective collision frequency of the reaction species at the interface of the catalyst. Furthermore, the kinetics of NaBH4 hydrolysis with the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst follows a zero-order kinetic model. Under microwave irradiation, the HGR of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst was 4.5 times that of pure Co3O4 at 40 °C. Incorporating carbon dots (CDs) into the Co3O4 matrix simultaneously enhances microwave absorption, increases hydrophilicity, and reduces the activation energy needed for breaking down O–H bonds in H2O. These improvements are expected to boost the overall catalytic performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is a viable chemical hydride for high-purity H2 production due to its numerous merits, including high H2 capacity (10.8 wt%), stability under ambient conditions, non-flammability, and non-toxicity [1–3]. Currently, H2 generation from NaBH4 hydrolysis typically uses various catalysts and conventional heating at low temperatures [4–6]. However, the use of microwave energy as a heating source to accelerate the reaction rate is rare. Among the catalysts studied, precious metals like platinum (Pt) and ruthenium (Ru) have demonstrated exceptional catalytic performance. For instance, Pt-based catalysts can achieve a hydrogen generation rate (HGR) of 18,000 mLH2 min−1 g−1 catalyst with an activation energy (Ea) of 40.9 kJ/mol [7], and Ru-based catalysts can achieve a HGR of 14,870 mLH2 min−1 g−1 catalyst with an activation energy (Ea) of 38.1 kJ/mol [8]. Despite their exceptional catalytic performance, these precious metals are expensive and not readily available, making them less feasible for large-scale applications compared to more abundant and cost-effective non-noble metals [4]. Therefore, using microwave energy with non-noble metal MW-responsive catalysts to expedite NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction rates holds significant potential.

Carbon dots (CDs, less than 10 nm in diameter) are an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials known for their exceptional physicochemical properties. These properties include a high microwave absorption capacity, with absorption efficiency reaching up to 90% of incident microwave energy under optimal conditions [9]. Their hydrophilicity is due to abundant surface functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH), with contact angle measurements as low as 32° [10]. Additionally, carbon dots exhibit excellent electron transfer capabilities, achieving a N
electron transfer value in the range of 3.0–4.0, which is crucial for efficient catalytic performance [11]. CDs also demonstrate effective synergistic interactions with metals, termed CDs-metal interactions, adjusting the active sites and enhancing active interfaces in catalysts designed for hydrogen production reactions [12]. Moreover, Co3O4 is an attractive option for the dehydrogenation of NaBH4 solution when exposed to microwaves, owing to its outstanding electromagnetic properties, cost-efficient manufacturing, and relatively high effectiveness [13,14]. Hence, developing a microwave-sensitive catalyst that incorporates both CDs and Co3O4 could be highly advantageous. This innovative catalyst has significant potential to harness the unique properties of CDs and Co3O4 under microwave energy, thereby greatly enhancing HGR during the dehydrogenation of NaBH4 solution.

In this study, we designed and synthesized a Co3O4 catalyst doped with carbon dots using coprecipitation of precursors, followed by calcination. The catalytic activities of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst for NaBH4 hydrolysis were assessed using both microwave and conventional heating. Additionally, the substantial influence of microwave energy on enhancing H2 production was examined through a specifically designed pseudo-homogeneous isothermal reaction setup. Furthermore, a comprehensive analysis of the promoting role of CDs for the catalytic activities of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst and the reaction mechanism was analyzed using experimental techniques and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

All chemicals used in this research, including multiwalled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (99.9 %), pure ethanol (C2H6O, ≥99.5 %), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, AR, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, ≥96 %), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98 %), were employed without further purification.

The procedure for preparing CD-doped Co3O4 commenced by dispersing CNTs (0.5 g) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (2 g) in pure ethanol (100 mL), employing sonication for a duration of 15 min. The resulting mixture was transferred into a 3-necked flask at room temperature and stirred magnetically at 1000 rpm. Subsequently, 50 mL of NaOH solution (1.36 g NaOH dissolved in 50 mL absolute ethyl alcohol) was added dropwise to the mixture solution over 10 min, controlled by a peristaltic pump. The solution was then allowed to stand for 12 h at room temperature with magnetic stirring at 800 rpm.

The solution was subsequently filtered, and the resulting sample was dried at 60 °C for 6 h in a vacuum. Following the drying process, the sample was subjected to calcination in air within a tube furnace at 700 °C for 2 h, utilizing a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Throughout this process, one end of the tube furnace remained closed until the temperature reached 700 °C. The product obtained at room temperature was Co3O4 doped with carbon dots, designated as CD-doped Co3O4. The synthesis process is shown as Fig. 1 .

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis using Cu-Kα radiation was performed with a Bruker D8 Advance instrument to investigate the crystallographic structure of the catalysts. Additionally, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI instrument to determine the valence states of the elements in both fresh and spent CD-doped Co3O4 catalysts. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were obtained within the 400–4000 cm−1 range using a Bruker Vertex 70 FTIR instrument. Contact angle measurements were conducted utilizing a DataPhysics OCA contact angle analyzer from DataPhysics, Germany.

The surface characteristics of the synthesized catalysts were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with an FEI Quanta 400 FEG instrument, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 instrument, and high-angle dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM). The elemental composition of the samples was determined using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX).

The configuration for microwave-enhanced hydrolysis of NaBH4 is illustrated in Fig. 2 (a). It should be noted that a programmable microwave device (UWave-2000, Sineo Microwave Chemistry Technology (Shanghai) Co) was utilized [15,16], in which the microwave irradiation to the reactor was operated in an on-demand mode rather than being continuous operated. This is to ensure that the desired reaction temperature throughout the entire reaction process was maintained. Therefore, for this study, a power setting of 400 W was selected to ensure sufficient heating capability.

Temperature monitoring was conducted using an infrared pyrometer (IR), delivering temperature readings from the side of the reactor surface, as depicted in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). To ensure accuracy in temperature measurement by an infrared pyrometer (IR), we established a pseudo-homogeneous solid-liquid reaction system with continuous magnetic stirring at 600 rpm throughout the reaction process. Continuous stirring strengthens heat transfer and quickly eliminates potential temperature gradients caused by the local hot spot phenomenon of microwave heating, resulting in an isothermal reaction system.

The quantity of produced H2 was determined by employing the approach of the displacement of water [14]. During the experimental process, an appropriate quantity of the prepared CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst was combined with an aqueous solution (90 mL) and subjected to ultrasound for 15 min. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was moved into a single-neck flask positioned at the core of the microwave chamber and continuously stirred by magnetic agitation at 600 rpm (as depicted in Fig. 2(a)). The system was programmed to sustain a specific dwell time at the desired temperature.

Upon reaching the desired temperature (e.g., 40 °C) within the catalyst-water mixture, an aqueous NaBH4 solution (30 mL) containing a specified NaBH4 concentration was introduced directly into the single-necked flask through a glass tube extending beyond the top of the microwave chamber. Simultaneously, the inlet for the NaBH4 solution was promptly sealed with sealing films, and mass recording started. The magnetic stirring speed was maintained at a constant 600 rpm in the microwave reactor throughout the entire process of NaBH4 hydrolysis. The recording interval was set at 30 s.

The rate of hydrogen generation (mLH2 min−1 g−1 catalyst) at a specific temperature was calculated according to (1) [17,18].

The Arrhenius equation was applied to calculate the activation energy (Ea) and pre-exponential parameter (A) across a temperature range of 35–55 °C for both MW and conventional heating methods during NaBH4 hydrolysis.

where A, Ea, R, and T denote the pre-exponential factor (L•min−1•g−1), activation energy (kJ•mol−1), gas constant (8.314 J mol−1•K−1), and reaction temperature (K), respectively. The k values (L•min−1•g−1) refers to the HGR at different temperatures [19]. The natural logarithm of k is plotted against the reciprocal of the reaction temperature (1/T).

Using VASP 5.4.4 with the Projected Augmented Wave approach, Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were conducted to optimize the geometric arrangements of Co3O4 and its surface reaction intermediates. Additionally, the assessment included the electromagnetic properties of both the bulk and surface configurations of Co3O4 [20].

The Perdew−Burke−Ernzerhof functional was used to model the energy of exchange-correlation [21]. To address the delocalization error from strong electron correlation in Co atoms, the Hubbard U method was employed with a U value of 3.32 eV for the 3d orbital of Co, sourced from the Materials Project database [22]. DFT-D3 was applied to incorporate van der Waals interactions [23]. For global optimization, cutoff energies were set at 520 eV for mass and 400 eV for the surface area of Co3O4, respectively. A k-point grid of 12 × 12 × 12 was used for the bulk configuration, and a 4 × 6 × 1 grid was used for the surface. To reduce periodic effects, a vacuum layer thickness of 15 Å was established. The structures were considered to have achieved full relaxation when the highest atomic forces were below 0.02 eV/Å, and the total energy difference within the electronic self-consistent field loop was less than 10−5 eV. To assess the properties of the active composites, the CD-doped Co3O4(110) surface configuration was constructed to represent the CD-doped Co3O4 structure. The hydrolysis of NaBH4 to generate hydrogen was simulated based on the CD-doped Co3O4(110) surface configuration.

In Fig. 3 (a), XRD patterns are presented for the different catalysts, including pure CNTs, Co(OH)2, CNT-Co(OH)2, pristine Co3O4, and Co3O4 doped with CDs. The XRD results for pure CNTs and Co(OH)2 were in good agreement with standard PDF #41–1487 and #51–1731, respectively. The XRD pattern for the CNT-Co(OH)2 composite reveals the coexistence of CNTs and Co(OH)2, indicating the successful preparation of the CNT-Co(OH)2 composite through the co-precipitation method. Upon calcination of Co(OH)2 and CNT-Co(OH)2, both the pure Co3O4 and the CD-doped Co3O4 exhibit identical planes such as (440), (511), (422), (400), (222), (311), (220) and (111). These planes correspond to 65.2°, 59.3°, 55.6°, 44.8°, 38.5°, 31.2° and 19.0° diffraction peaks, respectively (PDF #43–1003). This indicates that doping with CDs does not affect the crystalline structure of Co3O4. Furthermore, the absence of obvious diffraction signals from CDs in the CD-doped Co3O4 indicates the low doping amount, small size, and good dispersion of CDs in the structure [24,25].

XPS spectra were employed to determine the surface composition and chemical bonding states of the synthesized CD-doped Co3O4. In Fig. 3(b), the high-resolution Co 2p spectrum reveals the presence of two cobalt species in the fresh CD-doped Co3O4: Co2+ (780.3 [26], 780.6 [27] and 796.7 eV [28]) and hybrid (Co2+, Co3+) (779.3 [29], 794.3 [30] and 795.3 eV [28]). Additionally, satellite signals at 789.8 eV and 803.5 eV confirm the existence of both bivalent and trivalent cobalt ions in the CD-doped Co3O4 [31].

In the O 1s spectrum of the core-level (Fig. 3 (c)), three distinct peaks are observed at 529.6 eV, 530.5 eV, and 532.4 eV. These peaks correspond to different oxygen entities within the CD-doped Co3O4: lattice oxygen (Oa), oxygen species linked to surface oxygen vacancies (Ob), and adsorbed H2O (Oc), respectively. Furthermore, the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) exhibits three types of matched fitted peaks: C–C, C–O/C–O–C, and CO bonds, located at 284.7 eV, 286.0 eV, and 289.0 eV, providing evidence for the presence of CDs and –COOH groups bound to the surface of the CDs [32,33]. Additionally, the HAADF-STEM and EDX mapping images of CD-doped Co3O4 (Figs. 5 and 6
(g)-(k)) clearly show the existence of C, Co, and O. The uniform introduction of the C element into the Co3O4 matrix further supports the successful preparation of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst.

SEM (Fig. 4 (a)–(f)) and TEM (Fig. 5 (a)-(c)) analyses were carried out to explore the structure and morphology of the catalysts, including pure CNTs, prepared Co(OH)2, CNT-Co(OH)2 composite, pure Co3O4, CD-doped Co3O4, and spent CD-doped Co3O4. The surface of pure CNTs appears clean, while Co(OH)2 in its initial state consists of numerous clumped nanoparticles (Fig. 4 (a) and (b)). The SEM image of the CNTs and Co(OH)2 composite (Fig. 4 (c)) demonstrates that Co(OH)2 nanoparticles are uniformly anchored on the surface of the CNTs. Furthermore, the SEM and TEM images (Fig. 4 (d)–(e) and Fig. 5 (a)-(b)) reveal that after the calcination of Co(OH)2 and the CNT-Co(OH)2 composite, the pure Co3O4 has a nanosheet morphology, whereas the CD-doped Co3O4 exhibits irregularly shaped nanoplatelets with a smooth surface and a particle size ranging from about 50 to 500 nm. The size of CD-doped Co3O4 appears to be significantly larger than that of pure Co3O4, indicating that CNTs exert a substantial influence on shaping the size of CD-doped Co3O4 during the self-assembly procedure. This results in more active sites being exposed during the hydrolysis of NaBH4.

Furthermore, the HRTEM images of CD-doped Co3O4 reveal the uniform embedding of CDs within the Co3O4 matrix (Fig. 5 (d)). Four types of lattice fringes are observed (Fig. 5 (e)): 0.284 nm corresponding to the (101) spacing of the CDs and 0.233 nm, 0.244 nm, and 0.285 nm corresponding to the (222), (311), and (220) spacings of Co3O4, respectively. These findings confirm the successful incorporation of CDs into Co3O4 matrix. The interplanar spacings calculated from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image of CD-doped Co3O4 consist of seven diffraction rings (Fig. 5 (f)), including 0.143 nm, 0.155 nm, 0.202 nm, 0.233 nm, 0.244 nm, 0.285 nm, and 0.466 nm, corresponding to the (440), (511), (400), (222), (311), (220), and (111) planes of Co3O4 (JCPDS # 43–1003),
respectively. These results align consistently with the findings from X-ray diffraction (Fig. 3(a)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the optimal reaction condition
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal reaction conditions, NaBH4 hydrolysis over CD-doped Co3O4 under microwave irradiation was investigated at various factors, including the catalyst amount, the initial NaBH4 concentration, NaOH concentration. The specific reaction conditions used for investigating these parameters are outlined in Table S1.

As depicted in Fig. 6 (a), with the addition of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst within the range of 0.04–0.12 wt%, the completion time of NaBH4 hydrolysis reduced from 8 min to 4.5 min. Meanwhile, Fig. 6(b) shows a slight increase in the corresponding HGR of Fig. 6 (a) from 6951 mL/min/g with 0.04 wt% catalyst to 7107 mL/min/g with 0.08 wt% catalyst. The highest HGR was reached at 7107 mL/min/g with 0.08 wt% catalyst. However, when the catalyst concentration exceeded 0.08 wt%, there was a noticeable decrease in HGR. This is primarily because the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst was not fully utilized at concentrations above 0.08 wt%, as shown in Fig. 6(b) [34]. Therefore, 0.08 wt% of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst was selected for the subsequent experiments.

In Fig. 6 (c), it can be observed that the cumulative volume of H2 generated consistently rose across NaBH4 initial concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 1 wt%. However, in Fig. 6 (d), the associated HGR exhibited only marginal enhancement beyond an initial NaBH4 concentration surpassing 0.6 wt%. This phenomenon could be attributed to the increase in viscosity of the reaction solution, potentially giving rise to diffusion limitations thus appearing to lead to the deactivation of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst [35]. Hence, NaBH4 with an initial concentration of 0.6 wt% was selected for the next experimental process.

In Fig. 6 (e), as the quantity of NaOH increased, it became evident that the equilibrium time for NaBH4 hydrolysis escalated from 5 min (in the absence of NaOH) to 21.5 min (utilizing 8 wt% NaOH). Simultaneously, the HGR depicted in Fig. 6 (f) notably decreased from 7107 mL/min/g without NaOH to 5385 mL/min/g with 2 wt% NaOH. Moreover, the induction time extended with rising NaOH concentrations, ranging from 2 to 8 wt%. This suggests that the addition of NaOH adversely impacts the catalytic efficiency of the CD-doped Co3O4/NaBH4 reaction systems when exposed to microwaves. This observed impact is mainly attributed to the increasing concentration of OH− ions derived from NaOH, which impede the dissociation of H2O and render the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst inactive. This deactivation occurs as an increased presence of formed B(OH)4 - ions cover the surface of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst [36]. Therefore, based on these results, the experiments conditions without NaOH were used for the remaining experiments.

The impact of microwave thermal effect on the catalytic efficiency of CD-doped Co3O4 in converting NaBH4 to H2 was investigated across a temperature range of 35–55 °C. This exploration involved employing two distinct heating methods: microwave and conventional heating.

In Fig. 7 (a)-(e), the time to reach equilibrium in NaBH4 hydrolysis notably decreased with rising temperatures, demonstrating a considerable enhancement in the hydrolytic efficiency of NaBH4 under both heating methods. In addition, Fig. 7 (f) shows that the corresponding reaction rate of CD-doped Co3O4 steadily increased with increasing temperature. Higher temperatures promote the rapid movement of reactants, such as BH4 and H2O molecules, facilitating mass transfer during the hydrolysis of NaBH4 [37]. Notably, the HGR of CD-doped Co3O4 was consistently higher under microwave heating compared to conventional heating (Fig. 7 (f)). The increase in HGR ratio (indicated by the purple symbol-line in Fig. 8 (f)) also rose from 34.1% (at 35 °C) to 70.7% (at 55 °C) with MW heating, indicating that microwave energy significantly enhanced the reaction rate of NaBH4 hydrolysis.

This enhancement in HGR is primarily attributed to the selective heating of the synthesized CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst under MW irradiation. Selective heating creates localized hot spots on CD-doped Co3O4 surface within the NaBH4/CD-doped Co3O4 reaction systems [38,39]. Specifically, CDs and Co3O4 components can both be selectively heated using MW irradiation. The Co3O4 matrix, acting as catalytically active sites, absorbs electromagnetic energy, forming localized hot spots where the temperature is higher than the bulk temperature of the surrounding environment during NaBH4 hydrolysis under MW irradiation [40]. These hot spots significantly enhance the frequency of collision between reactants (BH4 − and H2O) and CD-doped Co3O4, leading to rapid hydrogen generation near these hot spots. Consequently, the overall rate of hydrogen production significantly increases compared to traditional heating methods [41], whilst appearing to be at the same bulk temperature. Moreover, the temperature rise rate of NaBH4 hydrolysis under MW irradiation is faster than conventional heating due to the synergistic effect of different heating mechanisms involving solid catalysts and liquid reactants. NaBH4 hydrolysis is heated through dipole rotations for CDs, magnetic loss for Co3O4, and dipole polarization for H2O under microwave irradiation. This multifaceted heating process significantly enhances hydrogen production from NaBH4 hydrolysis [39,42,43].

Moreover, the confirmation of localized selective heating under MW irradiation and its beneficial effect on the catalytic activities of CD-doped Co3O4 for NaBH4 hydrolysis were validated using a pseudo-homogeneous low-temperature reaction setup (see Fig. 2 (b)). This setup involved intermittent power-on and power-off of microwave energy over a 3-min duration. The reaction temperature was precisely maintained at 22 °C by employing a rapid continuous water flow, effectively minimizing the temperature rise effect caused by microwave irradiation. In these conditions, exclusive interaction of microwave energy occurred with the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst and the reaction pathways of NaBH4 hydrolysis, enabling H2 production at 22 °C. For comparative purposes, simultaneous experiments were carried out without microwave irradiation.

As shown in Fig. 7 (g), the entire reaction curves for NaBH4 hydrolysis exhibited notable differences when subjected to intermittent power-on and power-off microwave energy, in contrast to conditions without MW irradiation. Specifically, during the identical time periods of hydrolysis, under intermittent power-on and power-off microwaving, the total volume of produced H2 remained consistently higher compared to conditions without microwave irradiation. Moreover, the NaBH4 concentration (CNaBH4) continued to decrease as the hydrolysis process progressed, while the CNaBH4 at cycles of MW power-on and power-off was notably lower than that under non-microwave conditions at the same reaction point. For instance, the remaining CNaBH4 under MW power-on and power-off cycling was 8.8 % at the 1260 s reaction point, which was 31.5 % lower than that under non-microwave conditions (40.3 %). At the 1260 s reaction point (Fig. 7 (h)), the concurrent real-time HGR under MW power-on and power-off conditions surpassed that observed under non-microwave irradiation, measuring 836 and 701 mL/min/g, respectively. This indicates that despite significantly lower CNaBH4 concentrations, the kinetics of the hydrolysis reaction can still be accelerated. This is attributed to the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst being locally and momentarily heated during MW irradiation. Such localized heating can optimize heat utilization in the catalytic reaction, resulting in a higher HGR [44].

Furthermore, the real-time HGR (purple and blue symbol lines in Fig. 7 (h)) was consistently higher during MW irradiation than under non-microwave conditions (gray symbol line in Fig. 7 (h)), although the overall real-time HGR tended to increase as the catalytic process progressed. During the intermittent MW tests, the real-time rate of hydrogen generation was significantly enhanced during the MW irradiation phase, while this enhancement slowed down when the microwave was off. The real-time HGR curve appears to almost plateau from 900 to 1080 s when MW was turned off, while a noticeable upward trend in the real-time HGR curve was observed when MW was turned on again from 1080 to 1260 s. This implies that the localized selective heating induced by MW irradiation can counteract the detrimental impact of the relatively low CNaBH4 concentration, thereby enhancing the catalytic performance of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst.

In this research, an nth order kinetic model was employed to describe the reaction kinetics governing NaBH4 hydrolysis based on previously publications [45–47]. The experimental dataset of NaBH4 hydrolysis performed at 40 °C under distinct heating modes (Fig. 7 (b)) was selected as a representative case to ascertain the order (n) of reaction. Several equations associated with the nth order kinetic model were employed for this analysis [48]:

The reaction order values (n) for NaBH4 hydrolysis at 40 °C under traditional and microwave heating were calculated using Equation (5) and found to be 0.07 and 0.04, respectively. This suggests that the reaction order for NaBH4 hydrolysis catalyzed by the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst under different heating modes approximates a zero-order reaction [19].

Moreover, analyzing the NaBH4 hydrolysis curves at 40 °C under MW and traditional heating through zero (C0-Ct), first (lnC0-lnCt), and second (Ct −1-C0 −1) orders within the nth order kinetic model offers additional perspectives on the reaction order. Should changes in concentration values display a linear correlation with time, it signifies the adherence of NaBH4 hydrolysis to a particular reaction order [49,50]. In Fig. 8 (a) and (b), the exclusion of data points collected within the initial 120 s under MW heating and 270 s under conventional heating was necessary due to the low rate of NaBH4 hydrolysis during these time periods. For both traditional and MW heating conditions, linear regression analysis was executed on the remaining data points to evaluate the correlation between (C0-Ct), (lnC0-lnCt), and (Ct −1-C0 −1) values against time. The linear regression data for (C0-Ct), (lnC0-lnCt), and (Ct −1-C0 −1) at 40 °C under various heating methods are detailed in Table 1 . The findings reveal correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.986 and 0.979 for the zero-order under MW and traditional heating, respectively. These values notably surpass those for the first and second orders under distinct heating methods. As a result, the first and second orders exhibited poor linear fits with the data of (lnC0-lnCt) and (Ct −1-C0 −1). Hence, the zero order can be identified as the reaction order for NaBH4 hydrolysis at 40 °C under different heating methods [49–51], aligning with the outcome computed from the formula of the nth order kinetic model. This finding strengthens the evidence supporting a near zero-order reaction kinetics in this system.

Furthermore, the Arrhenius equation was utilized to confirm both the apparent activation energy (Ea) and the pre-exponential factor (A) across different heating modes within the temperature range of 35–55 °C. As depicted in Fig. 9 (a)-(d) and outlined in Table 3
, the activation energy under MW irradiation (50.08 kJ mol−1) was roughly 1.25 times greater than that derived from traditional heating (40.11 kJ mol−1). This contrast in activation energy values contradicts the findings typically reported in the literature [52]. The observed phenomenon can be attributed to the rapid activation and excitation of the hydrated Na + ion from the NaBH4 solution induced by MW irradiation throughout NaBH4 hydrolysis [53,54]. When solely considering the activation energy, microwave energy might seem disadvantageous for H2 generation from NaBH4 hydrolysis due to the elevated energy hurdle. However, in accordance with Collision Theory, the comprehensive reaction rate is dominated by parameters like collision frequency, molecular energy, and collision orientation. Table 2 illustrates that the A value attained through electromagnetic energy surpasses that acquired via traditional heating by approximately 67 times. This notable elevation in the A value indicates that MW irradiation substantially amplifies the frequency of effective collisions among the reaction species at the reaction interface of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst in comparison to conventional heating [55,56] i.e. the enhanced atomic mobility (observed with microwave heating) indicates an increase in the value of A [42,57,58]. Additionally, microwave energy was swiftly converted into both kinetic and intermolecular energy, promptly elevating the kinetic energy of reactants such as H2O molecules and BH4 −1 [59–61]. This process induces the weakening of O–H bonds within H2O and B–H bonds within BH4 − during the NaBH4 hydrolysis under microwave irradiation [62–64]. Consequently, there is an enhanced generation of H+ from H2O and H− from BH4 − under microwave irradiation, regarded as rate-determining steps governing the rate in NaBH4 hydrolysis [42,57,65]. The improved kinetic energy facilitates effective interactions between reactants, thereby accelerating the reaction rate. Therefore, the promotion in HGR under electromagnetic wave conditions can be ascribed to the elevation of both the pre-exponential factor and molecular kinetic energy.

To summarize, the catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 using a CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst exhibits a zero-order kinetic model under both MW and traditional thermal heating. Moreover, the efficacy of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst consistently surpasses that of conventional thermal heating when assisted by MW. This enhanced efficiency is attributable to the combined impact of both the thermal and non-thermal effects derived from MW energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Beneficial role of CDs for catalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the role of CDs in enhanced catalytic performance, a series of experiments under optimal reaction conditions were conducted using various catalysts, including pure CDs, Co(OH)2, CNT-Co(OH)2 composites, pristine Co3O4, and Co3O4 doped with CDs, under both microwave and conventional heating. the use of pure CDs as the catalyst leads to the production of only 183 mL of H2 under MW heating and 172 mL under conventional heating within 15 min. The corresponding HGRs were low at 54 mL/min/g and 48 mL/min/g, respectively, indicating that pure CDs are ineffective catalysts for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 under both conventional and microwave heating. It can be seen from Fig. 10 (b) and (c) that the hydrolysis of NaBH4 using pure Co3O4 as a catalyst took about 13.5 min under MW irradiation and 18 min under conventional heating, 8.5 and 12 min longer than that using the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst under the same conditions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10 (d), the HGR of pure Co3O4only increased by 14.6 % under microwave irradiation compared to conventional heating. In contrast, the HGR of CD-doped Co3O4 was 3.5 times higher than that of pure Co3O4 under conventional heating and 4.5 times higher under microwave irradiation. In addition, the HGR of CD-doped Co3O4 was enhanced by 46.8 % under microwave irradiation in comparison to that of 40 °C under conventional heating. Moreover, Fig. 10 (f) shows that CD-doped Co3O4 and pure Co3O4 exhibited superior catalytic activities compared with their precursors like CNT-Co(OH)2 composite and Co(OH)2. In particular, the HGR of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst was 7107 mL/min/g, which was16.9 and 4.5 times higher than that of the CNT-Co(OH)2 composite and pure Co3O4, respectively. These results indicate that the integration of CDs into the Co3O4 matrix and their synergistic interaction with microwaves significantly improves the catalytic performance of CD-doped Co3O4. Table 3 compares the HGR and Ea between published Co3O4-based catalysts and those prepared in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insights of the promotion role of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism behind the observed enhancement of the catalytic activities of CD-doped Co3O4 can be speculated as follows.

Firstly, the surface hydrophilicity of the Co3O4 catalyst was improved after the incorporation of CDs. According to the results of XPS C1s (Fig. 3 (d)) and FTIR (Fig. 11 (a)), hydrophilic functional groups such as –OH and –COOH remained on the surface of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst. Moreover, the quantity of –OH and –COOH groups on the surface of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst is larger than that of pure Co3O4, evident from the stronger FTIR peaks (Fig. 11 (a)) of –OH and –COOH groups in the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst compared to pure Co3O4. Additionally, the wetting properties of the Co3O4 catalyst doped with CDs and the pure Co3O4 were confirmed through water contact angle measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 11 (b) and (c), the Co3O4 catalyst doped with CDs has a contact angle of 9.6°, less than half of that of pure Co3O4 (19.9°). Consequently, the increased hydrophilicity of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst not only significantly enhanced the dispersion of the catalyst in NaBH4 solution but also facilitated the rapid access of reactants (BH4 − and H2O) to the surface of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst, enabling them to participate in dehydrogenation, thus leading to accelerated hydrogen production at the interfaces [37,72,73].

In addition, when CDs are incorporated into the Co3O4 matrix and exposed to microwave irradiation, the microwave absorption capacity of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst were enhanced compared to pure Co3O4. This enhancement is attributed to the introduction of CDs with a size less than 10 nm, leading to a high ratio of surface atoms and interfacial polarization. As a result, more microwaves can be absorbed and converted into heat on the surface of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst [9,74,75]. Consequently, the reaction rate between reactants such as BH4, BH3, BH2, BH, and H2O on or near the surface of the Co3O4 doped with CDs is significantly promoted, as the surface temperature of CDs is much higher than that of other domains [40,41].

Furthermore, the mechanism underlying the remarkable catalytic performance of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst was further analyzed through DFT calculations to elucidate the role of CD modification in the surface properties of Co3O4 on overall reaction pathways. Typically, the adsorption energy and the length of the corresponding bonds in the reactants and catalysts are considered critical factors for generating the target product. Based on this, the H2O adsorption behaviors were calculated using DFT, and the H2O adsorption configurations on the surface of pure Co3O4 and Co3O4 doped with CDs are shown in Fig. 12 (c). As depicted in the table inserted in Fig. 12 (c), DFT calculations indicate that the first O–H bond of the adsorbed H2O molecule on the CD-doped Co3O4 surface is 1.034 Å, which is longer than that on the pure Co3O4 surface (1.028 Å). This result confirms that the binding energy for the O–H bond in the H2O molecule is weaker on the Co3O4 surface doped with CDs, favoring the breaking of the O–H bond and the release of H radicals. Furthermore, the Co–O bond length between the O atom in the adsorbed H2O and the Co atom on the CD-doped Co3O4 surface was calculated to be 2.038 Å, which is shorter than the Co–O bond on the pure Co3O4 surface (2.063 Å). This result indicates a stronger interaction between the adsorbed H2O molecule and the Co3O4 surface modified with CDs. Additionally, the H2O adsorption energy on the CD-doped Co3O4 surface is calculated to be −1.251 eV i.e. lower than the value of −1.001 eV on the original Co3O4 surface. The relatively low H2O adsorption energy suggests that the active sites of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst have a greater potential to preferentially adsorb H2O molecules and efficiently activate them on the CD-modified Co3O4 surface. This is advantageous for the release of acidic hydrogen atoms from water splitting.

To date, H2O dissociation has been considered the rate-determining step (RDS) for producing H2 from the catalytic NaBH4 hydrolysis [73,76,77]. In this study, the standard hydrogen electrode model was used to calculate the hydrogen evolution reaction. As depicted in Fig. 12 (b), the Gibbs free energy of *H on pure Co3O4 (0.81 eV) is more than 2.5 times that of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst (0.32 eV). This indicates that the Co3O4 catalyst doped with CDs has a lower energy barrier for cleaving the O–H bond in H2O, resulting in a significant enhancement in hydrogen production activity.

The overall reaction pathways with the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst are illustrated in Fig. 12 (a). Initially, NaBH4 was dissolved in water and ionized, forming BH4 − species, which were subsequently adsorbed as *BH4 − on the surface of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst. Subsequently, the four hydrogen atoms (H−) resulting from the dissociation of BH4 − react sequentially with four acidic hydrogen atoms (H+) from the adsorbed H2O molecules to produce four H2 molecules. Simultaneously, OH− species derived from H2O bind successively with the adsorbed B atom to form B(OH)4 -, which then reacts with Na+ ions to produce unconstrained NaB(OH)4. The corresponding Gibbs free energy (Fig. 12 (a)) for each hydrolytic step is exothermic, indicating that the overall hydrolytic dehydrogenation of sodium borohydride is a spontaneous reaction, consistent with previous work [78].

Based on these results, the exceptional catalytic performance of the synthesized CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst is attributed to the combined effects of its adjusted intrinsic surface properties and the thermal and non-thermal effects of microwave irradiation.

To test the reusability of the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst, five cycles of recyclability testing were conducted. As shown in Fig. S1 (a) and (b), the HGR experience a continuous decrease from 7107 mL/min/g of the first run to 1887 mL/min/g of the 5th run. The reduction in HGR is mainly due to the lower degree of crystallization (see XRD pattern in Fig. S2) and surface aerosolization of the CD-doped Co3O4 (see SEM and TEM images in Fig. S3).

This study combined microwave energy and carbon dot doping to enhance hydrogen production efficiency from NaBH4 hydrolysis. Using microwave irradiation, the rate of H2 generation catalyzed by the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst increased from 34.1 % at 35 °C to 70.7 % at 55 °C, compared to conventional heating. The observed increase can be attributed to the synergistic influence of both microwave thermal and non-thermal effects, which accelerate the temperature rise and increase the frequency, movement, and mobility of collisions among reactant molecules and the catalyst. Selective heating by microwave irradiation was confirmed with a specially designed experimental setup. Furthermore, the kinetics governing NaBH4 hydrolysis, catalyzed by the CD-doped Co3O4 catalyst, followed a zero-order kinetic model.

Moreover, the role of carbon dots in microwave-enhanced NaBH4 hydrolysis was investigated through experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. At 40 °C under microwave irradiation, the HGR catalyzed by Co3O4 doped with carbon dots was 4.5 times higher than that of pure Co3O4. This improvement can be attributed to the introduction of carbon dots into the Co3O4 matrix, which simultaneously increases the absorption capacity for microwaves, enhances the hydrophilicity of the catalyst, and lowers the energy barrier required for the rate-determining step of H2O dissociation during NaBH4 hydrolysis. Therefore, our work provides a promising strategy for the design and fabrication of efficient Co3O4-based catalysts for hydrogen production from chemical hydrides.
